Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.

You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: I need to check what happened after this LEFT action. The white strip was at col 20, and the charcoal square is at cols 16-19. After moving LEFT, the white strip should be at col 16, overlapping with the charcoal square.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current state
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    b = n['boundary']
    rows = [p[0] for p in b]
    cols = [p[1] for p in b]
    if n['color'] not in ['w', 'g']:
        print(f"color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, bbox=({min(rows)},{min(cols)})-({max(rows)},{max(cols)}), children={n['children']}")

# Compare with before
if last_transition:
    before_seg = last_transition.before_frame.segmentation
    print("\nBefore:")
    for n in before_seg['nodes']:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        if n['color'] not in ['w', 'g']:
            print(f"  color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, bbox=({min(rows)},{min(cols)})-({max(rows)},{max(cols)}), children={n['children']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.content:
I see the white strip is now at col 16, but the charcoal square is still at (28,16)-(31,19). The white strip is at rows 32-35, col 16. The charcoal square is at rows 28-31, cols 16-19. They don't overlap - the white strip is below the charcoal square!
The white strip is at rows 32-35, and the charcoal square is at rows 28-31. So the white strip is 1 row below the charcoal square. They don't overlap.
I need to move UP to get the white strip to overlap with the charcoal square. But earlier, UP had no impact. Let me check if UP works now that the white strip is in a different position.
Actually wait - the white strip is at rows 32-35, col 16. The charcoal square is at rows 28-31, cols 16-19. The white strip's top row is 32, and the charcoal square's bottom row is 31. So they're adjacent but not overlapping.
If I move UP, the white strip would move to rows 28-31, overlapping with the charcoal square. But earlier, UP had no impact. Let me try UP now.
Actually, looking more carefully at the positions:
- White strip: rows 32-35, col 16 (vertical)
- Charcoal square: rows 28-31, cols 16-19
- Player: rows 32-35, cols 17-19
The white strip is directly below the charcoal square. If I move UP, the white strip should move up 4 rows to rows 28-31, overlapping with the charcoal square.
But earlier, UP had no impact when the white strip was at row 32 (horizontal). Maybe UP works now that the white strip is vertical?
Let me try UP.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# White strip at (32,16)-(35,16), vertical
# Charcoal square at (28,16)-(31,19)
# White strip is directly below charcoal square
# UP should move white strip to rows 28-31, overlapping with charcoal
# Let me try UP
action(['UP'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
